1: Elisha sends one of the sons of the prophets to Ramoth-gilead to anoint Jehu. Ramoth-gilead (identified with Tell ar-Rumeith in the Gilead region of modern Jordan) was a strategic border fortress contested between Israel and Aram; it had been the site of Ahab's fatal battle (1 Kgs 22) and where Joram was wounded (2 Kings 8:28-29). A prophetic anointing in the military camp, not the palace, signals that this is a military coup authorized by divine commission.
2: The young prophet is to find Jehu son of Jehoshaphat son of Nimshi among the army officers, bring him to an inner chamber (ḥeḏer bəḥāḏer — literally room within room, i.e., an inner room away from witnesses), and anoint him. The secrecy is tactically necessary: a prophetic anointing in the officers' mess would trigger immediate confrontation with the commander.
3: The anointing formula: pour oil on his head and say 'Thus says YHWH: I anoint you king over Israel.' The messenger is then to flee without delay — the anointing is a one-way prophetic act; there is no negotiation or ceremony, only the word and departure. The economy mirrors the urgency of a military operation.
4: The young man goes to Ramoth-gilead. The officers are sitting together — the military council (a sereḏ or officer assembly) — when the prophet arrives and addresses Jehu directly in their presence, then withdraws to the inner chamber.
5: When Jehu follows to the inner room, the prophet pours oil and delivers the oracle: YHWH's anointing of Jehu over Israel as king, and the specific commission — strike the house of Ahab to avenge the blood of YHWH's servants the prophets, and all YHWH's servants killed by Jezebel. This connects to Elijah's complaint (1 Kings 19:10: 'they have killed your prophets') and the Naboth oracle (1 Kings 21:19-26).
6: The commission is grounded in covenant justice: Ahab's house is to be destroyed just as the houses of Jeroboam son of Nebat and Baasha son of Ahijah were destroyed. The dynastic-destruction pattern from 1 Kings is now being applied to the third apostate dynasty of the north; YHWH is consistent in the covenant sanctions he announced.
7: The specific charge against Jezebel: her killing of YHWH's prophets. The whole house of Ahab shall perish, and YHWH will make the house of Ahab like the houses of Jeroboam and Baasha — the two previous dynasties God destroyed. The oracle is a comprehensive death sentence on the Omride line.
8: The prophet then flees — exactly as instructed. The brevity and stealth of the anointing protect the prophet from immediate political retaliation and allow Jehu to control the timing of the coup himself.
9: Jehu returns to the officers who ask what the madman (hamməšuggāʿ) wanted. Məšuggāʿ (from šāgaʿ, to be mad) is the term Shemaiah calls Jeremiah in Jeremiah 29:26 — prophets were commonly dismissed as madmen; the officers' language reflects the low social estimation of ecstatic prophets among military professionals.
10: When Jehu reveals the oracle, the officers immediately affirm him: they take their garments (bigdêhem), put them under him on the bare steps, blow the trumpet, and declare 'Jehu is king!' The spontaneous use of garments as a makeshift throne and the trumpet as coronation signal reproduce the ad hoc royal acclamation of 1 Kings 1:33-40 (Solomon) without the court apparatus. This is a field coronation.
11: Jehu immediately moves to prevent intelligence reaching Jezreel before him. He gives orders that no one escape the city to go tell it in Jezreel — information control is the tactical precondition for a successful coup against a sitting court.
12: Jehu drives to Jezreel — Joram is recuperating there from his wound at Ramoth-gilead (2 Kings 8:29). The watchman on the Jezreel tower reports: a company is coming from the direction of Ramoth-gilead; then reports the messenger Joram sent is not returning; the watchman reports another rider dispatched to meet Jehu, and he also does not return.
13: Jehu is recognized from a distance by characteristic driving style: kəmišpat Yēhûʾ ḇen-Nimšî kî ḇəšiggāʿôn yinhag — 'it is like the driving of Jehu son of Nimshi, for he drives furiously.' Šiggāʿôn is cognate with šāgaʿ (madness); ironically the officers called the prophet a madman (məšuggāʿ) and the watchman identifies Jehu by his mad driving. The reputation for reckless speed defines his character.
14: Joram orders his chariot harnessed and rides out with Ahaziah king of Judah to meet Jehu. The two kings ride together — Joram the wounded, Ahaziah his nephew — without recognizing the danger.
15: They meet Jehu at the plot of Naboth the Jezreelite. The geographic detail is precise and theologically loaded — this is the very field Ahab seized after Jezebel arranged Naboth's murder (1 Kgs 21). The meeting point makes the execution a visible fulfillment of Elijah's oracle (1 Kings 21:19: 'in the place where dogs licked up Naboth's blood, shall dogs lick your own blood').
16: Joram asks: 'Is it peace, Jehu?' (hăšālôm Yēhûʾ). Jehu's answer is devastating: 'What peace (šālôm), so long as the harlotries of your mother Jezebel and her sorceries are so many?' The covenant term šālôm cannot coexist with Jezebel's cult practices; Jehu frames the coup as a matter of covenant incompatibility.
17: Joram turns to flee and calls to Ahaziah: mirmāh ʾAḥazyāhû — 'Treachery, Ahaziah!' He is telling his nephew that Jehu is a traitor, not a military report; Joram has just realized this is a coup, not a military return. Both kings flee in separate directions.
18: Jehu draws his bow with full strength and shoots Joram between the arms (bên zərōʿāyw — literally between the arms = through the chest); the arrow exits through the heart. Joram slumps in his chariot. The Hebrew is anatomically specific — this is not a battle wound but a precise execution shot.
19: Jehu commands Bidkar his aide: take up Joram and throw him in the plot of the field of Naboth the Jezreelite. He then recites the Elijah oracle from memory (1 Kings 21:19): 'Surely I saw yesterday the blood of Naboth and the blood of his sons, says YHWH; and I will repay you in this plot, says YHWH.' Jehu himself connects the execution to the prophetic word — the coup is executed as a fulfillment of covenant justice, not merely political ambition.
20: Ahaziah of Judah flees toward Beth-haggan; Jehu pursues him and orders him struck at the ascent of Gur, which is by Ibleam (Khirbet Bel'ameh, identified in the Jezreel Valley near the Dothan pass). Ahaziah is Ahab's grandson through his mother Athaliah (2 Kings 8:26) and is killed because he was part of the Omride network of alliance.
21: Ahaziah flees to Megiddo and dies there. He is taken by his servants in a chariot to Jerusalem and buried in his tomb with his fathers in the City of David. Despite being a casualty of the Jehu coup, he receives a royal Davidic burial — his status as a Davidic king is maintained even in death.
22: Jehu comes to Jezreel. Jezebel has painted her eyes with kohl (pûk — the antimony/lead sulfide cosmetic applied around the eyes as black liner; documented in Egyptian and Levantine art and texts from the 2nd millennium BCE onward) and adorned her head. She looks from the window — the classic 'woman at the window' motif known from Canaanite ivory carvings.
23: Jezebel calls down: 'Is it peace, O Zimri, murderer of your master?' The reference to Zimri is biting — Zimri assassinated Elah and reigned only seven days before being burned to death (1 Kings 16:9-18); Jezebel is comparing Jehu to Israel's shortest-lived coup leader. She is insulting him, not pleading — a defiant, composed death.
24: Jehu looks up at the window and asks: 'Who is on my side? Who?' Two or three eunuchs look out. These are palace eunuchs (sārîsîm), who administered access to the queen's quarters; their willingness to betray Jezebel on command shows how completely her authority has collapsed.
25: At Jehu's command — 'Throw her down!' — the eunuchs throw Jezebel from the window. Her blood spatters on the wall and the horses, and they trampled her. The death is violent, undignified, and public — precisely the opposite of royal burial.
26: When Jehu sends servants to bury her ('for she is a king's daughter'), they find only the skull, feet, and hands — dogs have eaten the rest. This fulfills Elijah's oracle (1 Kings 21:23: 'dogs shall eat Jezebel in the territory of Jezreel') with anatomical precision. The text of the oracle is then cited explicitly in v36.
27: Jehu notes the fulfillment: this is the word of YHWH that he spoke through his servant Elijah the Tishbite. The narrator and Jehu both function as interpreters of the prophetic word — Jehu understands himself as a prophetic fulfillment agent. His reference to Elijah as 'servant of YHWH' (ʿeḇeḏ YHWH) uses the highest prophetic honorific.
28: No burial can be performed for Jezebel in the proper sense. The remnants cannot even be gathered for a tomb. The woman who opposed YHWH's prophets and instigated Naboth's death receives the prophesied death of the covenant-breaker: no grave, no memorial, no dignity in death.
29: The narrator supplies the chronological synchronism for Ahaziah's accession (the eleventh year of Joram) — a technical note inserted here to clarify a date discrepancy with the accession notice in 2 Kings 8:25 (twelfth year), which most scholars explain by different counting conventions (accession-year vs. non-accession-year reckoning).
30: The coup is complete: three kings killed (Joram of Israel, Ahaziah of Judah, and Jezebel the queen mother), all prophetic oracles from Elijah fulfilled in sequence. Jehu's arrival in Jezreel as undisputed king opens the next phase: the administrative purge of the Omride network.
31: A summary notice: Jehu has killed all who remained of the house of Ahab in Jezreel, all his great men and his familiar friends and his priests — a comprehensive purge of the royal household and its religious establishment. No one from Ahab's inner circle survives in the northern capital.
32: Jehu also strikes all who remained of Joram's supporters — military and political allies of the Omride house who would resist the new dynasty. The scope of the purge establishes Jehu's control through elimination rather than assimilation of the previous court.
33: The geographic summary of the coup: Jehu went from Ramoth-gilead to Jezreel; the entire execution of the prophetic commission happened within the span of a single military march. The speed of the coup (one day, two kings, one queen mother) is itself the sign of divine authorization — YHWH prepared the ground.
34: The Elijah oracle sequence is complete: Joram (1 Kings 21:19) and Jezebel (1 Kings 21:23) have both received exactly the death-in-field and dogs-eat prophecies. The narrative notes these fulfillments twice (vv27, 36) to ensure the reader maps prophecy to event.
35: Summary of the battle at Ramoth-gilead context: Joram was wounded there fighting Hazael (2 Kings 8:28-29), which removed him from Jezreel to recover — the wound that created the window for Jehu's coup. Without Hazael's pressure on Ramoth-gilead, Jehu would have had no opportunity to slip away from the front with an anointing.
36: The fulfillment statement for Jezebel: zeh dəḇar YHWH ʾăšer dibber bəyaḏ-ʿaḇḏô ʾĒliyyāhû haṬṭišbî — 'this is the word of YHWH that he spoke by the hand of his servant Elijah the Tishbite.' The second citation of the Elijah oracle within the same chapter serves as the chapter's theological bracket — the coup is not political but prophetic fulfillment.
37: The carcass of Jezebel shall be like dung on the face of the field in the portion of Jezreel, so they shall not say 'This is Jezebel.' No identification, no memorial, no tomb — the anti-burial as anti-monument. In ancient Near Eastern thought, an unmarked death without burial was the ultimate disgrace; Jezebel who tried to kill the prophets of YHWH receives the death YHWH declared through his prophet.
